Exam Cheatsheet

← Study Guide
1 — Introduction 2 — Info Economy 3 — Info Systems 4 — ICT 5 — IT Strategy
1

Introduction

Information Hierarchy

Symbols
Raw characters / digits
Syntax (grammar rules)
Data
Symbols arranged by syntax
Semantics (meaning via context)
Information
Data + context
Pragmatics (actions & actors)
Knowledge
Networked info enabling action

Properties of Information

Immaterial
No physical form; not consumed through use
Value-creating
Direct benefits to whoever uses it
Not a free good
Costs money to produce & maintain
Context-dependent
Value changes with when/where used
Adjustable in value
Filtering, combining, enriching
Has quality dimensions
Completeness, correctness, timeliness, relevance
Easily copied
Duplicated perfectly without quality loss
Requires standardized encoding
UTF-8, JSON, PDF, etc.
Transportable at speed of light
Travels globally once digital

Shannon Information Theory

  • Self-information: I(x) = -log₂(P(x)) (bits)
  • Entropy: H = -Σ(pᵢ · log₂(pᵢ)) = minimum bits to compress
  • High entropy → high randomness → more bits needed

IS Components

Information System
Human
Machine
Hardware
Application
Data
Processes
Functions
Connections

Krcmar Model

Information Economy Demand, Supply, Usage · IT Users
Information Systems Data, Processes, Lifecycle · IT Specialists
ICT Storage, Processing, Comms · Supports IS
Governance Strategy · Governance · Processes · HR · Controlling · Security

CIO Evolution

AspectPreviouslyNow
Reports toCFOCEO (board member)
Perceived asCost factorStrategic innovation driver
FocusBack-office opsDriving innovation

5 Context Evaluation Questions

Evaluate any piece of information by asking:

  1. How was it created?
  2. When was it created?
  3. For whom was it created?
  4. Who created it?
  5. Why was it created?

Data stripped of context can be misleading or used to deceive.

Three Perspectives on Information

PerspectiveCore Idea
Process"I am being informed" — dynamic act of communicating between sender & receiver
Knowledge"Intangible, subjective, in my mind" — interpretation depends on prior knowledge
Thing"It can be stored & retrieved" — tangible, storable object (document, file). Most relevant to IT.

Layer-Oriented Viewpoint of IM

Information Application Business side: what information does the org need?
IC Systems Software: which apps process the information?
Infrastructure Hardware: storage, networks, servers

Upper layers send requirements down; lower layers provide support up.

2

Information Economy

Information Logistics (Augustin, 1990) — 5 Rights

  1. Right information
  2. Right time
  3. Right amount
  4. Right place
  5. Required quality

Picot's 3 Dimensions of Info Demand

Objective
Info Need
Subjective
Info Need
Available
Information
Demand

Obj. not Subj. → Training gap · Subj. not Obj. → Noise

Needed not Avail. → Info gap · Avail. not Needed → Overload

Stakeholder Levels

Strategic Senior management · Direction, KPIs
Tactical Middle management · Dept. performance
Operational Operational staff · Day-to-day tasks

Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton, 1992)

Financial How should we appear to shareholders?
Customer How should we appear to customers?
Internal Process What processes must we excel at?
Learning & Growth How do we sustain change & improvement?

Each: Objectives → Measures → Targets → Initiatives

Information Quality — 4 Categories

Contextual Currentness, Completeness, Appropriate Scope, Relevance
Presentational Understandability, Clarity, Uniformity, Unambiguity
Intrinsic Reliability, Correctness, Objectivity, Credibility
Access Accessibility, Maintainability

Source/Demand Matrix

Internal SourceExternal Source
Internal DemandEnterprise Systems (primary IM focus)Specialized info
External DemandPublications, PR, Investor relationsNot relevant for IM

Taxonomy

Hierarchical classification: set of labels L + relationships R = ⟨l₁, l₂⟩. Labels can be instances (individuals) or classes (categories).

Semantic Web (Berners-Lee)

URI / Unicode
▶
XML
▶
RDF Triples
▶
Ontologies

Enables cross-system linking via shared vocabularies.

Data Warehouse & ETL

Extract Pull from heterogeneous sources
▶
Transform Clean, deduplicate, standardize
▶
Load Store in warehouse

Outputs: OLAP Analysis, Reporting, Data Mining

Information Logistics Cycle

Demand What do stakeholders require?
▶
Sources Which sources are available?
▶
Resources How to transform into usable form?
▶
Supply How to deliver at the right time?

3 Approaches to Eliciting Needs

ApproachMethodExamples
SubjectivePersonal interpretation of the taskInterviews, wishlists
ObjectiveInter-subjectively validated interpretationStrategic analysis, process analysis
MixedCombine both (typically objective first, then subjective)Process analysis → interviews

Personas

Fictional individuals representing a user group. Built from empirical data (interviews, analytics), then clustered and prioritized.

Each persona captures: demographics, goals, frustrations, and motivations — providing concrete guidance instead of designing for a vague, "elastic" user.

Source vs. Resource

Source
  • Any origin of data
  • Discovered
  • A database, report, or person
Resource
  • Source managed for reuse
  • Engineered
  • Organized, indexed, maintained

3 Approaches to Organizing Resources

ApproachStructureExamples
HierarchiesTree-structured classificationTaxonomy
KeywordsFlat or semi-structured labelsThesaurus, Tags
GraphsNetwork of linked conceptsSemantic Web
3

Information Systems

3 Fundamental SE Decisions

#DecisionOptions
1Project or ServiceOne-time effort vs. ongoing operation
2Waterfall or IterativeSequential phases vs. repeated cycles
3Inhouse or OutsourceInternal teams vs. external providers

Project vs. Service

Project
  • Leader: Project Manager
  • Time: Fixed span
  • Justified: Re-justified periodically
  • Strength: Hypothesis testing, prototypes
  • Weakness: Poor knowledge transfer
Service
  • Leader: Service Owner
  • Time: Indefinite (ongoing)
  • Justified: By ongoing demand
  • Strength: Stability, continuous improvement
  • Weakness: Less suited for exploratory work

Waterfall → V-Model → Agile

  • Waterfall (Royce): sequential phases. Original included feedback loops between adjacent phases.
  • V-Model: extends waterfall — each construction phase paired with a test level. Test planning starts during design.
  • Agile: iterative, incremental, customer-focused.

Agile Manifesto — 4 Values

Individuals & interactions over Processes & tools
Working software over Comprehensive documentation
Customer collaboration over Contract negotiation
Responding to change over Following a plan

Scrum

Roles
Product Owner · Development Team · Scrum Master
Artifacts
Product Backlog · Sprint Backlog · Product Increment
Events
Sprint Planning · Daily Scrum · Sprint Review · Sprint Retrospective

Kanban

Core principle: Limiting WIP (Work in Progress) increases throughput. Board visualizes columns with WIP limits. No prescribed roles or fixed iterations.

DevOps Loop

Dev
Plan→ Discover→ Build→ Test
→
Ops
Deploy→ Operate→ Observe
↺ Continuous Feedback

"You built it, you run it"

DevSecOps — Shift Left

Security integrated into every phase (not a final gate). Starts at requirements & coding — catches vulnerabilities earlier = cheaper to fix.

RE Process Facets (3 Dimensions)

Time
Linear Iterative
Target
Market-oriented Customer-specific
Purpose
Exploratory Prescriptive

3 RE Process Types

TypeTimeTargetPurpose
Product-orientedIterativeMarketExploratory
ContractualLinearCustomerPrescriptive
ParticipatoryIterativeCustomerBoth

Stachowiak's 3 Model Properties

Mapping Represents something in the real world
Reduction Leaves out irrelevant details
Pragmatics Serves a specific purpose

System Boundary vs. Context Boundary

Context Boundary
Humans Processes Services/Data Other Systems
System (SuD)

UML Use Case Elements

ElementNotationMeaning
ActorStick figure / rectangleRole that interacts with system
Use CaseOval/ellipseFunctionality system provides
System BoundaryRectangleInside vs. outside the system
AssociationSolid lineActor participates in use case
IncludeDashed arrow + <<include>>Mandatory sub-behavior

Activity Diagram Elements

ElementNotation
ActionRounded rectangle
Initial nodeFilled circle
Final nodeCircled filled circle
Decision / MergeDiamond (guard conditions in [ ])
Fork / JoinThick bar (parallel execution)
SwimlanesPartitions showing who executes

UML Class Diagram Syntax

ClassName
Attributes visibility name : type
Operations visibility name(params) : returnType

Multiplicities

NotationMeaning
0..1Optional (zero or one)
1Exactly one
* / 0..*Zero or more
1..*One or more
n..mAt least n, at most m

Generalization / Inheritance

  • Subtype inherits all supertype attributes & associations
  • Instance of subtype IS instance of supertype
  • Notation: hollow triangle arrow from subtype → supertype

Visibility Operators

SymbolNameAccessible From
+PublicAll classes with access to package
~PackageOnly inside this package
#ProtectedThis class + all subclasses
-PrivateOnly this class

Noun-Phrase Method Steps

1. Identify noun phrases → candidate classes
▼
2. Filter out Redundant, Attributes, Outside scope, Operations, Vague, Wrong detail level
▼
3. Identify verb phrases → operations and/or associations

Model vs. Diagram

Model
  • Contains all elements & relations
  • Source of truth (hierarchical tree)
  • Can exist without any diagrams
Diagram
  • Visualizes a selection
  • Graphical view of part of the model
  • One element can appear in many diagrams

MBSE vs. Document-Based

AspectDocument-BasedMBSE
ArtifactDocument types & structureTypes, Relations, Traces
OrganizationSeparate specs managed by different peopleUnified model, shared info architecture
ConsistencyManual cross-referencingAutomatic traceability

Cockburn Use Case Template

Name
Short descriptive name
Goal in Context
"As a [role], I want [action], so that [benefit]"
Precondition
What must be true before the use case starts
Success End
What is true when the use case succeeds
Failed End
What happens when the use case fails
Main Scenario
Numbered steps for the happy path
Alternatives
Variations branching from specific steps

Inhouse vs. Outsource

Outsourcing
Paying another company to do part of your work
Offshoring
Basing work in a different (cheaper) country
Nearshoring
Offshoring to geographically close locations
Expected BenefitAchieved?Obstacle
Cost savingsNoStart-up overhead
CapacityPartialCommunication gaps
QualityNoKnowledge loss
SpeedNoBetter kept onshore
InnovationPartialFailure to respond quickly

Modeling Dimensions

Specification Model Conceptual Model
Domain Model System Model
Formal domain types & constraints
Code-gen ready architecture
Whiteboard real-world concepts
Whiteboard + implementation concepts

Domain = real-world concepts only · System = adds implementation concepts

12 Agile Principles

1. Early, continuous delivery
7. Working software = progress
2. Welcome changing requirements
8. Sustainable pace
3. Deliver frequently (prefer short)
9. Technical excellence
4. Daily business-dev collaboration
10. Simplicity (maximize work not done)
5. Motivated individuals + trust
11. Self-organizing teams
6. Face-to-face communication
12. Regular reflection + adjustment

System under Development (SuD) — 5 Properties

Operational context
Environment that influences or is influenced by the SuD
Context boundary
Separates operational context from non-relevant environment
Inner structure
Related elements composing the system
Interfaces
Distinguish SuD from its context; define the system boundary
Behavior
Observable at the interfaces

DevOps Tool Categories

CategoryGoal
Knowledge sharingCulture of collaboration, breaking silos
Source code mgmtVersioning, knowledge sharing
Build processRelease engineering, test automation, static analysis
Continuous IntegrationCI pipeline, artifact management
Deployment automationIaC, containerization, cloud services
Monitoring & LoggingRuntime monitoring, alerting

DevSecOps — Security by Phase

PhaseSecurity Activities
DevelopRequirements analysis, Secure coding
BuildSAST (Static Application Security Testing)
TestWhite box DAST, Black box DAST
ReleaseDigital signing
DeliverSecure transfer, Security analysis
DeploySecurity configuration, Security scanning
OperateSecurity patching
MonitorSecurity monitoring, Security auditing

Mental Models & Ubiquitous Language

Mental Model (Norman, 1983): organized understanding and mental representation of knowledge about key system elements.

If people hold different mental models of the same system → misunderstandings and defects.

Domain Driven Design (Evans, 2004): shared ubiquitous language between developers and domain experts so code reflects the domain model directly.

4

Information & Communication Technologies

Duncan's 4 IT Infrastructure Groups

Hardware Computers, servers, peripherals
Network & Telecom Cable, phones, video equipment
Data Databases, data warehouses
Core Data-Processing Apps ERP, CRM

CIO's 4 ICT Responsibilities

  1. Observe & evaluate technological developments
  2. Strategic planning of tech decisions
  3. Develop & protect competitively relevant technologies
  4. Internal & external exploitation of tech capabilities

Central question: Leader or Follower strategy?

Innovation Matrix

Do new things (20%) Do what we do, better (80%)
Existing Knowledge (90%) Leading-Edge (10%)
II — 8%
IV — 2%
I — 72%
III — 8%

Gartner Hype Cycle — 5 Phases

Innovation
Trigger Peak of Inflated
Expectations Trough of
Disillusionment Slope of
Enlightenment Plateau of
Productivity

Cloud Models Comparison

SaaS Fully serviced software · You manage: nothing
BaaS Ready-made backend · You manage: frontend
PaaS Serviced system · You manage: apps, data
IaaS Serviced hardware · You manage: OS, runtime, apps

↑ Higher = less control, less to manage

VM vs. Container

VM
  • Via: Hypervisor
  • OS: Full OS each
  • Weight: Heavy
  • Isolation: Strong (separate OS)
  • Use: Full environments, legacy
Container
  • Via: Container Runtime
  • OS: Shared host kernel
  • Weight: Lightweight
  • Isolation: Process-level
  • Use: Microservices, APIs

Distributed Systems

Core challenge: failure of unknown machines can render your system unusable. Design for resilience: redundancy, graceful degradation, circuit breakers.

Three Cloud Service Layers

Application Services Content mgmt, Enterprise software, Collaboration, Process mgmt
Platform Services Block storage, Communication networks, Identity mgmt, Content servers
Infrastructure Services Computing resource mgmt, Network mgmt, Storage mgmt
5

IT Strategy

Business-IT Relationship

Business
defines goals for →
← transforms
IT

Extended: Business Context (competition, market, regulation) ↔ Business ↔ IT

Earl's 3 Strategy Types (1989)

IS Strategy — What? Business applications of IT, aligning with business needs
IT Strategy — How? Technology architecture, standards, security, risk
IM Strategy — Who? Where? Structures & roles for managing IS and IT

Porter's Five Forces

Threat of New Entrants
Bargaining Power of Suppliers
→
Industry Rivalry
←
Bargaining Power of Customers
Threat of Substitutes

Porter's Generic Strategies

Industry Segment Industry-wide
Singularity Cost Advantage
Focus (Differentiation)
Focus (Cost)
Differentiation
Cost Leadership

4th Strategy: Customer & Supplier Intimacy — use IS to develop strong ties & loyalty

Resource-Based View (RBV) — 3 Levels

Vision C-level · Strategic direction
Capabilities Middle management · Adapting resources
Resources Product & project mgmt · Assets, tech, culture

VRIN Criteria (all 4 required)

V Valuable Provides value to the firm
R Rare Not widely available
I Inimitable Difficult to copy
N Non-Sub. No equivalent substitute

SWOT Matrix (MBV + RBV Bridge)

External (MBV) Internal (RBV)
Helpful Harmful
Opportunities
Threats
Strengths
Weaknesses

SAM — Henderson & Venkatraman, 1993

Internal External
Business IT
Org. Infrastructure Admin, processes, skills
IS Infrastructure Architectures, processes, skills
Business Strategy Scope, competencies, governance
IT Strategy Tech scope, systemic competencies

Strategic Fit: External ↔ Internal · Functional Integration: Business ↔ IT

4 Alignment Perspectives

PerspectiveDriverTop Mgmt RoleIS Mgmt RolePerformance
Strategy ExecutionBiz StrategyStrategy FormulatorStrategy ImplementorCost/Service Center
Technology TransformationBiz StrategyTech VisionaryTech ArchitectTech Leadership
Competitive PotentialIT StrategyBiz VisionaryCatalystBiz Leadership
Service LevelIT StrategyPrioritizerExecutive LeadershipCustomer Satisfaction

3 Core IT Management Questions

IT management balances strategic advantage through IT investments vs. short-term cost control:

  1. Which IT services should be provided?
  2. Who provides these IT services?
  3. Are the IT services provided correctly (efficiently & effectively)?

Basic Strategy 101 — 5 Steps

1. Identify Business strategy and goals
▼
2. Break down Goals into concrete activities & processes
▼
3. Metrics Identify metrics to measure progress
▼
4. IT contribution Identify how IT can help progress
▼
5. Measure Measure actual performance

IT Strategy Document Contents

  • Competitive analysis & forecasting
  • Mission statement, goals & basic tasks
  • Strengths/weaknesses of own ICT & market tech
  • Assessment of future technology development
  • Hardware and software policy
  • Priority rules for IT projects
  • Organizational developments
  • Guidelines, standards & norms
  • Personnel development in IT

Tangible vs. Intangible Resources

Tangible
  • Factories, hardware, money
  • Strategy: keep up to date
Intangible
  • Skills, culture, creativity, patents
  • Strategy: develop capabilities & vision

Extends the RBV: intangible resources are harder to imitate → stronger VRIN advantage.

Support Tools for Strategy Steps 2–5

BSC
Balanced Scorecard — links strategy to 4 perspectives
KPIs
Key Performance Indicators — quantifiable measures of success
OKRs
Objectives & Key Results — goal-setting framework
GQM
Goal–Question–Metric — derive metrics from goals via questions
